REW_BEN_038.target_semantics: Multi-select benefits question: would need one share-of-employers-offering-X per option; no single positive pole exists at row level.
REW_BEN_038.verbatim_evidence: Prevalence ALL-UK vs LARGE(250+) vs SME: PMI/health insurance 18% all / 70% large / 17-18% SME; Occupational sick pay 18% all / 60% large
REW_BEN_038.flags: CONTEXT not a single prevalence: this anchor is a compendium of ~15 separate benefit prevalences (each with its own all/large/SME split), i.e. a distribution across options of a multi-select question. Usable only if split into one structured row per benefit option; several entries are themselves ranges (e.g. PMI SME 17-18%) or large-only (e.g. free eye tests 52% large). Do not collapse to one number. Note internal near-duplicates with other rows (e.g. OSP 60% large here vs 60-66% in REW_BEN_SICK_001; EAP 10% all / 61-62% large here vs 67/78/39 in REW26_WEL_EAP - conflicting EAP figures across register rows, reviewer should reconcile sources).

REW_BEN_046.target_semantics: Share of employers offering group income protection / long-term disability cover (to all employees); positive pole = Yes, offers income protection.
REW_BEN_046.verbatim_evidence: Group income protection / long-term disability: 25% all; 40% large(250+); 15% SME (offered to all employees)
REW_BEN_046.flags: Rates are on an 'offered to all employees' basis - employers offering IP to only some staff are excluded, so these slightly understate any-offer prevalence. Pairing check: SME=15, large=40; 25 is the ALL-UK rate, not the SME rate.

REW_BEN_048.target_semantics: Would describe the modal salary-replacement level among employers that offer IP (a benefit-level distribution), not a share of all employers offering anything; no prevalence pole.
REW_BEN_048.verbatim_evidence: among UK employers offering IP, 50-59% of salary is the most common replacement level (25.2%). NB 34% of employers offer no IP at all
REW_BEN_048.flags: CONTEXT not a prevalence: 50-59% is a benefit LEVEL band (percent of salary), and 25.2% is the share of the conditional base (employers already offering IP) sitting in that modal band - conditional distribution, not an all-employer target share. The 34% 'offer no IP' figure is corroboration context only and conflicts in direction of expression with REW_BEN_046 (25% offer vs 34% offer none - different sources/bases, broker-book skew per base_field). Grade B, not nationally weighted.

REW_BEN_HOL_001.target_semantics: Share of employers giving full-time staff 25+ days annual leave excluding bank holidays; positive pole = 25+ days (above the ~20-day floor).
REW_BEN_HOL_001.verbatim_evidence: 25+ days annual leave (excl bank hols), FT: 36% all; 58-59% large; 35% SME
REW_BEN_HOL_001.flags: RANGE MIDPOINT: prose says '58-59%' for large; large_pct=58.5 is the midpoint and does NOT appear verbatim - validator will not string-match it, reviewer must accept the derivation. Pairing check: SME=35, large=58.5; 36 is ALL-UK, not SME.

REW_BEN_SICK_001.target_semantics: Share of employers providing occupational sick pay above statutory SSP; positive pole = has occupational sick pay scheme (above-statutory).
REW_BEN_SICK_001.verbatim_evidence: Occupational sick pay: 18% all (Fig13); 60-66% large; 17-18% SME
REW_BEN_SICK_001.flags: TWO RANGE MIDPOINTS: large '60-66%' -> 63; SME '17-18%' -> 17.5; neither midpoint appears verbatim, so the validator will flag both - reviewer must accept the derivations. SME midpoint (17.5) sits just below all-UK (18), which is arithmetically tight but plausible given large-employer weighting. Cross-row: REW_BEN_038 compendium quotes '60% large' for the same measure (bottom of this range).

REW263_WEL_FINWELL.target_semantics: Share of employers with a documented financial wellbeing strategy/policy (beyond ad-hoc activity); positive pole = has documented strategy.
REW263_WEL_FINWELL.verbatim_evidence: Financial wellbeing strategy/policy: 15% all orgs; 53% large have-or-plan
REW263_WEL_FINWELL.flags: The 53% large figure is 'have-or-plan' - a different measure (have OR plan to have) that is NOT comparable to the 15% 'have' rate, so it is deliberately excluded from large_pct rather than mis-paired; classified all_only, not all_large. Also note sibling row REW26_WEL_FINWELL carries 64% for a broader 'any programme/activity' measure - different construct (programme activity vs documented strategy), do not reconcile them to one number.

REW26_WEL_EAP.target_semantics: Share of employers providing an Employee Assistance Programme (offered to all employees); positive pole = Yes, provides an EAP.
REW26_WEL_EAP.verbatim_evidence: EAP: 67% all employees; 78% large(250+); 39% SME (offered to all). NB HW Fig16=67% all
REW26_WEL_EAP.flags: PAIRING TRAP row: SME=39 pairs with large=78; 67 is the ALL-UK rate, NOT the SME rate. Duplicate figure in same prose ('NB HW Fig16=67% all') is consistent, no conflict. Wording '67% all employees' most plausibly means all-UK employer rate on an offered-to-all-employees basis, but could be misread as % of employees covered - reviewer should confirm employer denominator. Cross-row conflict: REW_BEN_038 compendium cites 'EAP 10% all / 61-62% large' from a different source - materially different; reviewer must pick the source of record.

REW26_WEL_SCREENING.target_semantics: Share of employers offering health screening / health assessments (offered to all employees); positive pole = Yes, offers health screening.
REW26_WEL_SCREENING.verbatim_evidence: Health screening: 35% all; 47% large(250+); 23% SME (offered to all)
REW26_WEL_SCREENING.flags: Rates are on an 'offered to all' basis - any-offer prevalence may be slightly higher. Pairing check: SME=23, large=47; 35 is ALL-UK, not SME.

REW_BEN_112.target_semantics: Would assert the share of employers whose BASE employer pension contribution falls in the 3-6% band — a distribution band over a level question ('typical contribution'), not an offer/not-offer prevalence; no binary positive pole exists.
REW_BEN_112.verbatim_evidence: "Base employer contribution: 68% of employers sit between 3% and 6% of pensionable salary (Aon Q77/78)"
REW_BEN_112.flags: CONTEXT only: 68% is the share of employers inside a 3-6% contribution RANGE — a distribution statistic for a categorical level question, likely spanning multiple answer options, so unusable as a single target share. Broker-book skew toward larger/advised employers per base_field. Grade B.

REW_BEN_PENS_EE_MAX_01.target_semantics: No share-of-employers figure exists at all — the prose gives median/quartiles/range of the max employee DC contribution RATE (a value/level distribution); nothing here can be asserted as a target share.
REW_BEN_PENS_EE_MAX_01.verbatim_evidence: "Max employee DC contribution: median 6% (p25 5%, p75 7%), range 3-12% (n=59)"
REW_BEN_PENS_EE_MAX_01.flags: CONTEXT only: all figures are contribution-rate LEVELS (median 6%, p25 5%, p75 7%, range 3-12%), not employer-prevalence percentages — the % signs denote salary percentages, not shares of employers. Tiny base (n=59) with large-employer/FTSE skew and pre-2024 vintage per base_field. Grade B.

REW_BEN_PENS_EMP_MAX_01.target_semantics: Would assert the share of employers whose MAX employer pension contribution is >=9% (or >=11%) — cumulative distribution points over a level question, not a binary offering prevalence; no single positive pole maps to the question's option set.
REW_BEN_PENS_EMP_MAX_01.verbatim_evidence: "~50% of employers offer max employer contribution 9%+; 27% offer 11%+ (Aon Q77/78)"
REW_BEN_PENS_EMP_MAX_01.flags: CONTEXT only: two OVERLAPPING cumulative thresholds (~50% at 9%+, 27% at 11%+; the 11%+ group is a subset of the 9%+ group) — these are CDF points for a banded level question, not option shares, and cannot seed a single marginal without the reviewer choosing a threshold. '~' approximate on the 50. Broker-book skew toward larger/advised employers per base_field. Grade B.

REW_FAI_STUDY_TIME_93b6ef22.target_semantics: Share of all-UK employers offering paid study leave/time; positive pole = 'yes, paid study time is provided' for the question 'Is paid study time provided for employees undertaking formal qualifications'.
REW_FAI_STUDY_TIME_93b6ef22.verbatim_evidence: "Paid study leave/time is offered by ~70% of UK employers" | "92% offer training/career-development, 75% pay professional subscriptions/fees"
REW_FAI_STUDY_TIME_93b6ef22.flags: '~70%' approximate — extracted as 70. CONSTRUCT PROXY: prose's own NOTE says 70% measures paid study LEAVE and protected paid study TIME (day-release) 'may be somewhat lower — treat as upper bound'; base_field repeats this. Usually targeted to certain employees rather than universal. The 92% (training/career-dev) and 75% (professional subscriptions) figures are OTHER benefits — do not pair with the target. Small base (~280 reward professionals, CIPD Oct 2021). DUPLICATE ANCHOR: same underlying CIPD Oct 2021 70% figure as REW_BEN_FAM_009 — two metrics will share one source number.

REW262_TIME_BEREAVEMENT.target_semantics: Share of employers offering paid bereavement leave above statutory, excluding parental bereavement; positive pole = 'yes, we offer paid bereavement leave above the statutory minimum'.
REW262_TIME_BEREAVEMENT.flags: TAXONOMY GAP: prose gives ALL (27%) AND SME (26%) but NO large rate — no all+SME base_type exists, so classified all_only on the 27% all-UK figure with sme_pct=26 recorded for the reviewer. Not sme_large (no large rate to pair). Implied large rate sits slightly above 27% given SME at 26% and SME employment weight — reviewer may wish to back-derive rather than lose the SME figure. Grade A.

REW_BEN_FAM_001.target_semantics: 33% = share of UK employers offering statutory-minimum maternity pay ONLY — this is the NEGATIVE pole of the question ('at statutory' vs 'enhanced'); the enhanced-pay positive side is the unstated complement (~67%, derived, NOT written in prose).
REW_BEN_FAM_001.verbatim_evidence: "33% of UK employers offer statutory-minimum maternity pay only; 18% offer 4-13 wks enhanced at/near full pay; 21% offer 26 wks enhanced at/near full pay then 13 wks SMP. (Private 38% statutory-only vs public 13%; public most generous.)"
REW_BEN_FAM_001.flags: POLARITY WARNING: 33% is the statutory-ONLY share — do NOT read as the share offering enhanced pay. Prose is really a partial categorical distribution (33% statutory-only, 18% enhanced 4-13wks, 21% enhanced 26wks — sums to 72%, incomplete) matching a multi-option question; only the statutory-only share is a clean single target. Sector splits present (private 38% / public 13% statutory-only) — large-generosity/sector skew. Pre-dates ERA 2025 reforms per base_field; baseline expected to shift. Grade A, n=2,000 weighted.

REW_BEN_FAM_007.target_semantics: Share of UK employers offering PAID carer's leave specifically; positive pole = 'yes, paid carer's leave provided' (the statutory Carer's Leave Act 2023 right is UNPAID, so paid provision is above-statutory).
REW_BEN_FAM_007.verbatim_evidence: "PAID carer's leave specifically offered by ~36% (RMS 2022) / 31-45% (flex 2025 support data)" | "52% of UK employers have a policy/procedure for employees to request carer's leave (75% public, 46% private, 59% voluntary; 67% large 250+ vs 33% SME)"
REW_BEN_FAM_007.flags: PAIRING/CONSTRUCT TRAP: the 67% large / 33% SME / 52% all splits measure a DIFFERENT construct — having a policy/procedure to REQUEST carer's leave (which is unpaid statutory) — NOT paid provision. Do NOT build sme_large from 33/67 for this paid-leave question. Paid figures conflict across sources: ~36% (RMS 2022) vs 31-45% range (2025 flex data, midpoint 38); extracted 36 because it is the explicit point estimate appearing verbatim (midpoint 38 does not appear in prose). '~' approximate. Sector splits (75/46/59) also belong to the request-policy construct. Grade A base, but the paid point estimate itself is older RMS 2022.

REW_BEN_FAM_009.target_semantics: Share of UK employers offering paid study leave; positive pole = 'yes, we provide paid study or exam leave'.
REW_BEN_FAM_009.verbatim_evidence: "70% of UK employers offer paid study leave" | "92% offer training/career-development, 75% pay professional subscriptions, 75% conference attendance"
REW_BEN_FAM_009.flags: Often grade/role-dependent rather than universal — prevalence of offering to ANYONE, not universal entitlement. The 92% (training/career-dev), 75% (professional subscriptions) and 75% (conference attendance) figures are OTHER L&D benefits — do not pair with the target. Private-skewed (70%) modest sample per base_field (~280 reward professionals, item bases ~212-253, CIPD Oct 2021). DUPLICATE ANCHOR: same underlying CIPD Oct 2021 70% figure as REW_FAI_STUDY_TIME_93b6ef22 — reviewer should note two metrics anchor on one source number.

REW_BEN_FAM_010.target_semantics: Share of all-UK employers offering paid volunteering leave; positive pole = 'Yes, we provide paid volunteering leave' (paid time off for voluntary/community work).
REW_BEN_FAM_010.verbatim_evidence: 30% of UK employers offer paid time off for voluntary/community work
REW_BEN_FAM_010.flags: Base per register: ~280 reward professionals (item bases ~212-253), private-skewed 70% — modest sample. Anchor's 'minority, above-statutory practice' note is qualitative context only.

REW_BEN_SICK_002.target_semantics: None usable as a target share — this is a distribution of full-pay OSP duration bands among orgs that have OSP, not a share of employers doing X; CONTEXT only.
REW_BEN_SICK_002.verbatim_evidence: OSP duration full pay: ~10% <1 month; ~40% >6 months; public sector most generous
REW_BEN_SICK_002.flags: NOT a prevalence: duration distribution (~10% under 1 month, ~40% over 6 months) with conditional denominator 'orgs with OSP', approximate '~' figures, bands do not sum to 100. Question itself asks a duration ('for how long'), not a yes/no. 'Public sector most generous' is qualitative. Route to CONTEXT.

REW_BEN_SICK_004.target_semantics: Share of orgs WITH OSP whose staff are eligible from day one, i.e. NO waiting period — the question's 'yes, there is a waiting period' side is the COMPLEMENT (~70%); the 30% attaches to the no-waiting-period pole.
REW_BEN_SICK_004.flags: CONDITIONAL DENOMINATOR: base is orgs with OSP (per register base_field), NOT all-UK employers — 'all_only' here means single-rate-over-stated-base; generator must condition on the OSP parent. Figure is approximate ('~30%'). POLARITY INVERTED vs question wording — do not seed 30% as the 'waiting period exists' share. '43% public' is a public-sector split, not extracted.

EXT_REW_GAP_009.target_semantics: Share of UK employers (for roles supporting remote work) that require a minimum number of office days; positive pole = 'yes, a mandatory office-day minimum exists' vs no requirement.
EXT_REW_GAP_009.verbatim_evidence: 65% of UK employers require a minimum number of office days
EXT_REW_GAP_009.flags: Only the headline 65% is extracted. The day-count split (3 days 48%, 2 days 32%, 4 days 14%, 1 day 8%) is CONDITIONAL on the 65% ('Among those') — never treat those as marginals. '5-10 days (26%)' by-month and '14% of hybrid employers plan to introduce/increase' are context/forward-looking. Question is frequency-shaped ('how often'); 65% answers only the any-requirement split, with the conditional distribution as shape context. Strong base: n=2,050, weighted, nationally representative (CIPD/YouGov 2025).

OT_04_b14623a6.target_semantics: Share of UK employers paying a shift premium for unsocial hours; positive pole = 'Yes, shift premiums are paid'.
OT_04_b14623a6.verbatim_evidence: 63% of employers pay a shift premium
OT_04_b14623a6.flags: LARGE-SKEW BASE: register base_field says large-employer/FTSE skew, answered-only n, pre-2024 vintage — prose says 'employers' with no size qualifier, so treat 63% as large-skewed rather than a clean all-UK rate. Grade B. Premium-structure parenthetical (fixed-rate most common; some % of hourly) is qualitative context.

PROP_634adacd.target_semantics: None usable as a target share — 3% is a median pay-award LEVEL (a value employers award), not a share of employers doing X; CONTEXT only.
PROP_634adacd.verbatim_evidence: Median expected basic pay increase 3% overall, across all sectors (private/public/voluntary)
PROP_634adacd.flags: NOT a prevalence: 3% is the median EXPECTED basic pay increase (forward-looking level/value). Seeding it as an employer share would be a category error. Route to CONTEXT for the salary-increase question's answer distribution.

REW26_WEL_FINWELL.target_semantics: Share of UK employers offering a financial wellbeing programme/activity (broad any-programme measure); positive pole = 'Yes, we offer a financial wellbeing programme'.
REW26_WEL_FINWELL.flags: Register relabel history: ruled 2026-07-16 that this anchor is the broad 'programme/activity' measure (previous 'policy/strategy' mislabel; audit mis-map OVERTURNED) — anchor attachment is correct as-is, but source wave still to be tightened to the exact CIPD survey carrying S29. Base described only as 'mixed UK', not a clean all-UK denominator. Broad/generous definition — expect the rate to run high vs stricter policy measures.

REW26_WEL_STRATEGY.target_semantics: Share of UK employers with a stand-alone documented wellbeing strategy; positive pole = 'Yes, a documented wellbeing strategy exists' (the question's review-frequency tail is not covered by this rate).
REW26_WEL_STRATEGY.flags: Size figures in prose are band EXTREMES, not SME/large(250+) rates: '75% at 1000+' (subset of large, would overstate a 250+ rate) and '21% at 2-49' (excludes 50-249, would understate an SME rate) — deliberately NOT extracted into large_pct/sme_pct to avoid band mismatch; use as size-gradient context/shape only. Only the 57% all-UK rate is a clean target share.

EXT_REW_GAP_004.target_semantics: Share of employers operating a long-service award scheme; positive pole = Yes (operates a scheme).
EXT_REW_GAP_004.flags: Base_field warns of large-employer/FTSE skew and answered-only n, so this single rate likely overstates SME prevalence — treated as all-UK only because no size split exists in prose; pre-2024 vintage; grade B.

PAYTR_01_42eae7ec.target_semantics: Share of UK employers whose job adverts TYPICALLY include a salary or salary range; positive pole = Yes (salary/range typically included).
PAYTR_01_42eae7ec.flags: Two candidate cuts in prose: ~40% 'always' vs 53% 'always-or-sometimes'. Chose 40 because the question says 'typically', which is closer to 'always' than to 'sometimes'; reviewer may prefer 53. The 40 is written as approximate ('~40%'). Same underlying stat as REW262_GOV_PAYINADVERTS, where I chose the 53 cut for its unqualified 'do you include' wording — confirm the pair is intentional.

PROP_10d1211d.target_semantics: Share of employers that ran a disability pay gap analysis in the past year; positive pole = Yes (ran the analysis).
PROP_10d1211d.verbatim_evidence: 18% of UK employers ran disability pay gap analysis in year to Oct 2023 (27% of large 250+ employers had done so vs 9% of SMEs
PROP_10d1211d.flags: Pairing-trap shape: 18 is the ALL-UK rate, NOT the SME rate — blend pair is SME=9 / large=27. The trailing '46% of large had NOT, 28% didn't know' are complement/don't-know context, not prevalence targets (27+46+28=101 due to rounding, large don't-know share is big). 'No legal requirement currently' is qualitative context only.

PROP_930043cc.target_semantics: Share of UK employers that carry out ethnicity pay gap analysis; positive pole = Yes (carries out the analysis).
PROP_930043cc.flags: The '(10% share)' is the subset who SHARE/disclose results, not the carry-out prevalence — do not use as target. 18 carry-out + 56 have-not = 74, so ~26% unaccounted (presumably don't know/planning). 'Main barrier company size' is qualitative context; base is size-skewed in behaviour but no numeric size split given.

REW262_GOV_PAYINADVERTS.target_semantics: Share of UK employers that include pay or a pay range in job adverts at least sometimes; positive pole = Yes (includes pay/range).
REW262_GOV_PAYINADVERTS.flags: Two candidate cuts: ~40% 'always' vs 53% 'always-or-sometimes'. Chose 53 because this question is an unqualified 'Do you include...', so any inclusion counts; the stricter 40 ('always', written as approximate '~40%') is the alternative. Same underlying stat as PAYTR_01_42eae7ec, where I chose 40 for its 'typically' wording — reviewer should confirm the two metrics are meant to sit at different cuts.

REW263_GOV_UKPAYTRANS.target_semantics: Share of UK employers publishing salary ranges always-or-sometimes — a binarized cut of a categorical 'how would you describe your pay-transparency position' question; positive pole = the option-side(s) indicating ranges are published (always/sometimes) vs not.
REW263_GOV_UKPAYTRANS.verbatim_evidence: 53% publish ranges always/sometimes; only 16% publish both ranges+specifics
REW263_GOV_UKPAYTRANS.flags: Question is categorical ('How would you describe...'), not yes/no, so 53 only works as a target if the option set binarizes cleanly into publishes-ranges vs not — reviewer must confirm the option map; if it cannot be binarized, downgrade to CONTEXT. The 16% ('publish both ranges+specifics') is a stricter top-band cut, usable only for a top-option target; 'only 16%' is rhetorical phrasing. Overlaps the same underlying 53% stat as PAYTR_01/REW262.

REW_FAI_079.target_semantics: Share of UK employers that carry out gender pay gap analysis; positive pole = Yes (conducts the analysis).
REW_FAI_079.verbatim_evidence: 44% carry out (30% always+14% sometimes share); mandatory 250+; public sector 52%; 79% of <250 had NOT
REW_FAI_079.flags: NOT sme_large despite size info: the large rate is given only as 'mandatory 250+' (de jure ~100%, no number in prose) and the sub-250 rate only negatively ('79% of <250 had NOT', implying ~21% positive — NOT stated verbatim, so cannot be output). Parenthetical 30+14 decomposes the 44 into always/sometimes SHARING of results, not separate carry-out rates. 'Public sector 52%' is a sector split, context only. Large-vs-SME behaviour differs hugely here — flag for blend logic since mandatory 250+ means the 44 all-UK rate masks a near-ceiling large rate.
